paradoxes.name: Executive Function Paradox
paradoxes.description: Consumers purchase cognitive or health supplements to solve executive dysfunction or tracking fatigue. However, the product requires high executive function to track, optimize, and customize, leading to subscription abandonment when the user lacks the mental capacity to sustain the feedback loops.
paradoxes.validation: Optimize by eliminating customer tracking burdens. Auto-locking formulas and moving from active daily logging to passive check-ins should show an immediate reduction in cohort cancel rates.
paradoxes.example: A customized nootropics system requiring users to fill out detailed daily spreadsheets tracking focus metrics, resulting in users canceling because they 'feel overwhelmed' by the self-optimization process.

paradoxes.name: Margin Subsidy Trap
paradoxes.description: Brands mask structural retail or supply-chain losses by driving highly aggressive DTC customer acquisition. The cash generated by DTC sales subsidizes retail-level distribution overheads and slotting fees, leading to aggregate capital depletion despite high top-line growth.
paradoxes.validation: Contribution margin isolation tests. Modeling and segregating pure DTC margins from retail-distribution overheads to identify direct contribution rates before ad spend.
paradoxes.example: A nutrition bar brand growing DTC revenue to cover slotting fees and high distributor chargebacks on retail shelves, realizing too late that every dollar of retail scale is draining capital.

paradoxes.name: Routine Friction
paradoxes.description: Friction occurs when a product's preparation or consumption ritual requires more physical steps than the consumer's established morning or evening routine can absorb. Without habit cues and physical-friction reduction, subscribers build up excess inventory and cancel.
paradoxes.validation: Physical preparation time trials. Tracking daily usage compliance relative to preparation step counts (e.g. jar scoop mixing vs single-serve travel pack).
paradoxes.example: A supplement brand requiring users to scoop powder, mix with warm liquid, and frothe in the kitchen. When users travel or are rushed, they break the habit, bags accumulate, and subscriptions cancel.

paradoxes.name: Recovery Attribution Paradox™
paradoxes.description: Brands misallocate marketing spend by crediting direct-response ads for subscription growth, failing to realize that high customer acquisition costs (CAC) are subsidizing low-retention cohorts who only purchase due to temporary discount incentives.
paradoxes.validation: Cohort LTV vs CAC ratio analysis. Segmenting cohorts by acquisition offer and tracing pure contribution margin payback curves.
paradoxes.example: A health brand scaling Meta ad spend based on a 2.0x click-ROAS, failing to notice that the acquired customers carry a 60% Day-30 churn rate, rendering them unprofitable.

paradoxes.name: Ritual Fragmentation
paradoxes.description: A product establishes strong adherence within a highly specific physical environment (e.g., kitchen counter at home). When the user's environment changes (travel, office days, weekends), the habit sequence fragments, resulting in supply backlog and cancellations.
paradoxes.validation: Travel-cohort behavior tracking. Introducing modular travel accessories to a randomized cohort and measuring the impact on product backlog delays.
paradoxes.example: A mushroom coffee brand that fits into a home morning ritual. When the user travels for a week, they leave the tub behind, skip 7 days of consumption, return to a backlog of unopened powder, and delay their next subscription order.

paradoxes.name: Personalization Decay
paradoxes.description: The premium perceived value of a 'personalized formulation' degrades rapidly once a customer identifies their favorite option. If the platform continues to force optimization surveys or active changes, the personalization transitions from a benefit into navigation friction.
paradoxes.validation: Survey interaction elasticity. Tracking retention differences between subscribers prompted to constantly adjust their formulas versus those locked into fixed favorites.
paradoxes.example: A custom protein brand forcing subscribers to re-evaluate their recipe every month, triggering choice fatigue and cancellation rather than providing simple, passive refills.

paradoxes.name: Attribution Dilution Trap
paradoxes.description: Marketing ad spend driving multi-channel conversions is evaluated under single-channel metrics. Meta/Google ads drive significant traffic, but users drop off and purchase on Amazon or retail. Single-channel analytics report a failing ROAS, leading teams to cut ad spend, which collapses overall brand volume.
paradoxes.validation: Media Mix Modeling (MMM) correlation. Performing incremental ad-spend holdouts and tracking direct correlation with Amazon and retail sales spikes.
paradoxes.example: A clean nutrition brand running Meta ads that appear unprofitable on Shopify, failing to see that 35% of ad viewers buy the product via Amazon Prime, diluting Shopify-reported ROAS.
